name: Dystopian Fiction
description: Chilling visions of the future from the great dystopian novels, where surveillance, conformity, and control reveal what we stand to lose.
author: Quips Editorial

quotes.content: War is peace. Freedom is slavery. Ignorance is strength.
quotes.notes: The three paradoxical slogans of the Party, emblazoned on the Ministry of Truth.

quotes.content: Big Brother is watching you.
quotes.notes: The caption beneath the ubiquitous posters of the Party leader, embodying the surveillance state.

quotes.content: Who controls the past controls the future: who controls the present controls the past.
quotes.notes: A Party slogan on the malleability of history that Winston records in his diary.

quotes.content: If you want a picture of the future, imagine a boot stamping on a human face—for ever.
quotes.notes: O'Brien's vision of perpetual power, spoken during Winston's interrogation in the Ministry of Love.

quotes.content: Freedom is the freedom to say that two plus two make four. If that is granted, all else follows.
quotes.notes: Winston writes this in his diary, defending objective truth against the Party.

quotes.content: We shall meet in the place where there is no darkness.
quotes.notes: O'Brien's words to Winston, which Winston wrongly takes as a promise of solidarity.

quotes.content: Perhaps a lunatic was simply a minority of one.
quotes.notes: Winston's reflection on how dissent from official reality is branded as madness.

quotes.content: All animals are equal, but some animals are more equal than others.
quotes.notes: The revised commandment painted on the barn wall after the pigs seize power.

quotes.content: The creatures outside looked from pig to man, and from man to pig, and from pig to man again; but already it was impossible to say which was which.
quotes.notes: The novel's closing sentence, as the pigs become indistinguishable from the humans they overthrew.

quotes.content: Man is the only creature that consumes without producing.
quotes.notes: From Old Major's rousing speech that sparks the animals' rebellion.

quotes.content: Community, Identity, Stability.
quotes.notes: The World State's motto, mounted over the door of the Central London Hatchery.

quotes.content: But I don't want comfort. I want God, I want poetry, I want real danger, I want freedom, I want goodness. I want sin.
quotes.notes: John the Savage's defiance of Mustapha Mond, claiming the right to be unhappy.

quotes.content: Everyone belongs to everyone else.
quotes.notes: A hypnopaedic slogan conditioning citizens against private attachment.

quotes.content: Ending is better than mending.
quotes.notes: A sleep-taught slogan promoting consumption over repair.

quotes.content: A gramme is better than a damn.
quotes.notes: A conditioning slogan urging the use of the drug soma to suppress discontent.

quotes.content: It was a pleasure to burn.
quotes.notes: The novel's opening line, narrating fireman Guy Montag's delight in burning books.

quotes.content: Stuff your eyes with wonder, he said, live as if you'd drop dead in ten seconds.
quotes.notes: Granger relays his grandfather's counsel to Montag near the novel's close.

quotes.content: We need not to be let alone. We need to be really bothered once in a while.
quotes.notes: The retired professor Faber argues for the value of being challenged.

quotes.content: There must be something in books, things we can't imagine, to make a woman stay in a burning house; there must be something there.
quotes.notes: Montag, shaken after a woman chooses to die with her books, begins to doubt his work.

quotes.content: Nolite te bastardes carborundorum.
quotes.notes: Mock-Latin for "don't let the bastards grind you down," which Offred finds scratched in her closet by a previous handmaid.

quotes.content: Better never means better for everyone. It always means worse, for some.
quotes.notes: The Commander justifies the regime of Gilead to Offred.

quotes.content: We were the people who were not in the papers. We lived in the blank white spaces at the edges of print.
quotes.notes: Offred recalls the willful complacency of life before Gilead.

quotes.content: There is more than one kind of freedom, said Aunt Lydia. Freedom to and freedom from.
quotes.notes: Aunt Lydia reframes oppression as protection during the handmaids' indoctrination.

quotes.content: Is it better for a man to have chosen evil than to have good imposed upon him?
quotes.notes: The prison chaplain frames the novel's central question about free will and forced virtue.

quotes.content: What's it going to be then, eh?
quotes.notes: The opening line, narrated by Alex, that recurs to begin each of the novel's three parts.

quotes.content: Ralph wept for the end of innocence, the darkness of man's heart, and the fall through the air of the true, wise friend called Piggy.
quotes.notes: Near the close of the novel, Ralph grasps the savagery that consumed the marooned boys.

quotes.content: What I mean is... maybe it's only us.
quotes.notes: Simon haltingly suggests that the dreaded beast is the boys' own nature.

quotes.content: The worst part of holding the memories is not the pain. It's the loneliness of it. Memories need to be shared.
quotes.notes: The Giver explains to Jonas the burden of bearing a community's memories alone.

quotes.content: Memories, even your most precious ones, fade surprisingly quickly. But I don't go along with that. The memories I value most, I don't ever see them fading.
quotes.notes: Kathy H., the narrator and a clone raised for organ donation, clings to memory against loss.
